skill.name: sql-patterns
skill.description: Reference guide for SQL patterns, including CTEs, joins, pagination, window functions, and indexing strategies.
skill.summary: Find practical SQL examples for analytical queries and database performance tuning.
skill.author: 0xDarkMatter

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many shell execution, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns. Manual review found these are false positives caused by Markdown SQL fences, SQL hash index terminology, ranking functions, DESC ordering, and PostgreSQL catalog statistics examples. No prompt injection, malicious intent, network access, credential access, or executable code was found.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in SQL Reference Content
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown fenced SQL examples, not executable commands. The weak cryptography and reconnaissance labels match SQL terms such as hash indexes, ranking functions, DESC ordering, and PostgreSQL statistics views used for query tuning guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are visible Markdown SQL examples and documentation tables. I found no executable scripts, shell commands, network calls, or credential handling in the reviewed files.

content.user_title: Optimize SQL Queries with Proven Patterns
content.value_statement: SQL users often need correct patterns for analytics, pagination, joins, and indexes. This skill provides concise examples and guidance for faster, clearer database queries.
actual_capabilities: Provides examples for common table expressions and chained CTEs.
Explains window functions such as ranking, lag, lead, moving averages, and frames.
Compares join types and pagination approaches for practical SQL use.
Summarizes index types including B-tree, hash, GIN, GiST, composite, partial, and covering indexes.
Shows query analysis patterns with EXPLAIN, EXPLAIN ANALYZE, and PostgreSQL statistics views.
Lists common SQL anti-patterns and safer query rewrites.
limitations: It is a reference guide, not a live database analyzer.
Examples are generic and require adaptation to each schema and SQL dialect.
It does not benchmark queries or inspect production database plans automatically.

use_cases.title: Write Analytical Queries
use_cases.description: Use window function examples to calculate rankings, running totals, moving averages, and period comparisons.

use_cases.title: Improve Slow Queries
use_cases.description: Use indexing and EXPLAIN guidance to choose better indexes and identify costly scans.

use_cases.title: Teach SQL Patterns
use_cases.description: Use concise examples to explain CTEs, joins, pagination, and common query mistakes.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Basic Pattern
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner lookup for a common SQL task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the SQL pattern for a CTE that filters active users before joining orders.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Window Function
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to rank products by monthly revenue and handle ties. Which window function should I use?

prompt_templates.title: Plan Indexes for a Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Given a query filtering by customer_id, status, and created_at, suggest a practical composite index strategy.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose Query Anti-Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my SQL query for pagination, wildcard search, functions on indexed columns, and missing index risks.

output_examples.input: I need daily revenue with yesterday comparison and running total.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend LAG for the previous day value and SUM over an ordered window for the running total.

output_examples.input: My query filters active users by email and status.
output_examples.output: The skill would suggest a partial or composite index, depending on selectivity and whether status is always present.

output_examples.input: I need pagination that works well for large tables.
output_examples.output: The skill would compare offset pagination with keyset pagination and explain when keyset pagination is more scalable.

best_practices: Adapt each example to your SQL dialect, table size, and data distribution.
Use EXPLAIN or EXPLAIN ANALYZE before adding indexes to production systems.
Prefer explicit columns, selective predicates, and stable ordering in reusable queries.
anti_patterns: Do not copy indexes without checking whether the query planner uses them.
Do not use leading wildcard searches when a normal B-tree index is expected to help.
Do not rely on OFFSET pagination for large result sets when keyset pagination fits the workflow.

faq.question: Does this skill connect to my database?
faq.answer: No. It provides reference examples and guidance. It does not connect to databases or inspect live schemas.

faq.question: Which SQL dialect does it target?
faq.answer: The examples are broadly SQL-oriented, with several PostgreSQL-specific indexing and statistics examples.

faq.question: Can it write complete production queries?
faq.answer: It can help draft patterns, but users must adapt names, predicates, permissions, and performance constraints.

faq.question: Does it cover window functions?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes ranking, navigation functions, running totals, moving averages, frames, and practical analytical examples.

faq.question: Does it explain index tradeoffs?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers index types, composite order, covering indexes, partial indexes, expression indexes, and maintenance checks.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. Manual review found only SQL documentation examples and no executable code, prompt injection, or data exfiltration behavior.
